data.content_ref: inline:{"claim": "Egypt survived because its centralized temple-state redistributive apparatus was structurally antifragile compared to peer-polity palace systems","mechanism": "The New Kingdom combined pharaonic ideology with temple estates (Amun, Ptah, Ra) that maintained parallel surplus networks; when one node failed, others compensated, unlike Mycenaean wanax-systems or Hittite vassal networks that collapsed through single-point administrative failure","scope": {"covers": ["Egypt", "Mycenaean Greece", "Hittite Anatolia"], "excludes": ["Ugarit", "Levantine city-states"]}, "forbidden": [{"case": "Egyptian temple estates show simultaneous collapse and centralization under Ramesses III, not mutual buffering", "eval": "rubric:std-hist"}, {"case": "Ugarit had multiple merchant guilds and parallel administrative structures analogous to Egyptian temple networks", "eval": "rubric:std-hist"}, {"case": "The Egyptian state under Ramesses III experienced precisely the same invasion pressures but with identical institutional responses to peer polities", "eval": "rubric:std-hist"}], "prose_notes": "Institutional-comparison candidate; deliberately atypical in applying Taleb-style antifragility to Bronze Age. Requires Egypt as controlled exception."}
